interactions.summary: Jiang contrasts Roman and Carthaginian war capacity, narrates Pyrrhic and Punic wars, then attacks the historicity of Cannae by applying military logic, archaeology, and comparative battle models.

claims.claim: Greek specialized forces can win battles but wither, while Rome wins wars by replenishing poorer citizen soldiers.
Carthage loses because wealth lets it outsource war to mercenaries, while Rome’s poverty forces citizens into invested, cohesive warfare.
Jiang argues Cannae is suspect because ancient surrounded armies should become energized, the tactic lacks ancient parallels, and no archaeological site has been found.

signature_moments.moment: Rome loses battles but keeps winning wars.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Romans don’t actually win that many battles, but they win most of their wars
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Condenses Jiang’s model of Roman military endurance.

signature_moments.moment: Cannae is treated as too perfect to be trusted.
signature_moments.source_phrase: This battle strategy makes no sense
signature_moments.why_it_matters: Turns a canonical military triumph into a diagnostic problem.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Pyrrhus wins battles at a cost that shows the fragility of specialized Greek military forces.
The encirclement model Jiang uses to question Cannae’s plausibility.

chronology_notes.note: The Second Punic War and Hannibal story are introduced as famous but later subjected to Jiang’s source-skeptical framework.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript says Battle of Canaan where context indicates Cannae; preserve source wording in semantic refs.
